diagrams.title: JButton Class Inheritance Hierarchy
diagrams.description: Shows the complete class hierarchy of JButton from Object to JButton, demonstrating how Swing components are built on AWT foundations
elements: Object (java.lang package) - Root class at top
java.awt.Component - Base for AWT graphical components
java.awt.Container - Can contain other components
javax.swing.JComponent - Base for all Swing components
javax.swing.AbstractButton - Base class for buttons
javax.swing.JButton - Final concrete button class at bottom
Arrows pointing downward showing 'extends' relationship
Package labels: java.lang, java.awt, javax.swing
diagrams.purpose: Students understand that JButton inherits from AbstractButton (not directly from Component), learning how Swing components are layered on AWT and the significance of the javax.swing package

diagrams.title: JButton Model-View-Controller (MVC) Architecture
diagrams.description: Internal architecture showing how JButton separates data (model), display (view), and logic (controller) following the MVC design pattern
elements: JButton (Component) - The visible button user interacts with
ButtonModel (Model) - Stores state: pressed, selected, enabled, actionCommand
Look and Feel (View) - Determines visual appearance
ActionListener (Controller) - Handles user interactions
Arrow: JButton delegates to ButtonModel for state
Arrow: ButtonModel fires ActionEvent to ActionListener
Arrow: Look and Feel renders based on Model state
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the MVC connection critical to Swing, understanding how button state is managed separately from its display and event handling, enabling flexible UI customization

diagrams.title: JButton Event Handling Flow
diagrams.description: Step-by-step flow diagram showing the event-driven programming model when a user clicks a JButton
elements: User clicks JButton (starting point)
ButtonModel receives press event
ActionEvent object created with source, actionCommand, timestamp
Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) receives event
Event queue processes the ActionEvent
ActionListener.actionPerformed() invoked
Application code executes in handler
Arrow: User → ButtonModel → Event → EDT → Listener → Handler
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete event lifecycle in Swing, learning about the Event Dispatch Thread and how user interactions translate to method calls in their code

diagrams.title: JButton States and Transitions
diagrams.description: State diagram showing the different visual and logical states a JButton can have and what triggers transitions between them
elements: Default state (normal appearance)
Rollover state (mouse hovers over button)
Pressed/Armed state (mouse button held down)
Disabled state (button not interactive)
Focused state (button has keyboard focus)
Arrows labeled with trigger events: mouseEnter, mousePressed, mouseReleased, setEnabled(false), requestFocus
diagrams.purpose: Students learn that JButton has multiple states beyond just enabled/disabled, understanding how visual feedback is provided to users and how to programmatically control button behavior

diagrams.title: JButton Constructor Variations
diagrams.description: Shows the four JButton constructors with their parameters and what each creates
elements: JButton() - Empty constructor: Creates button with no text or icon
JButton(String text) - Text-only: Creates button with displayed text
JButton(Icon icon) - Icon-only: Creates button with image icon
JButton(String text, Icon icon) - Combined: Creates button with both text and icon
Arrows showing text appears center, icon appears left (default)
diagrams.purpose: Students learn all four ways to instantiate JButton and when to use each constructor based on UI requirements

charts.title: AWT Button vs Swing JButton Comparison
charts.content: Comparison table with rows: Package (java.awt vs javax.swing), Thread Safety (not EDT-safe vs EDT-safe), Look and Feel (OS-dependent vs pluggable), Features (limited vs rich styling), Mnemonic support (no vs yes), Tooltip (no vs yes), Borders (simple vs customizable), Icons (no vs yes)

charts.title: JButton Common Methods
charts.content: Table with columns: Method, Return Type, Description. Rows: setText(String), getText(), setIcon(Icon), getIcon(), setActionCommand(String), getActionCommand(), addActionListener(ActionListener), removeActionListener(ActionListener), setEnabled(boolean), isEnabled(), setMnemonic(int), setToolTipText(String), setRolloverIcon(Icon)

charts.title: Creating Interactive JButton Steps
charts.content: Flowchart: 1. Import javax.swing.* → 2. Create JButton instance → 3. Add to Container (JPanel/JFrame) → 4. Create ActionListener implementation → 5. Register listener with addActionListener() → 6. Implement actionPerformed() with logic → 7. Run on Event Dispatch Thread

charts.title: JButton vs Other Swing Button Types
charts.content: Comparison of JButton with JToggleButton, JCheckBox, JRadioButton: JButton is momentary (click and release), JToggleButton maintains state, JCheckBox for boolean options, JRadioButton for mutually exclusive selections

branches: Class Hierarchy - extends AbstractButton → JComponent → Container → Component → Object
Constructors - JButton(), JButton(String), JButton(Icon), JButton(String, Icon)
Key Methods - setText(), setIcon(), setActionCommand(), setMnemonic(), setToolTipText()
Event Handling - ActionListener, actionPerformed(ActionEvent), event-driven model
States - Default, Rollover, Pressed, Disabled, Focused
Integration - Added to JPanel, JFrame, uses LayoutManagers, Look and Feel
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of JButton: its inheritance, creation, configuration, event handling, internal architecture, and integration into Swing applications

branches: Button Family - JButton, JToggleButton, JCheckBox, JRadioButton, JMenuItem
Event Model - ActionEvent, ActionListener, Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)
UI Delegates - ButtonUI, manages painting and rendering
mindMaps.purpose: Shows how JButton fits into the larger Swing component ecosystem, highlighting relationships with related button types and the shared event handling infrastructure
